sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Delirium: prevention, diagnosis and management in hospital and long-term care
sources.version: NICE clinical guideline CG103, last updated 18 January 2023
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; requires UAE legal and pathway adaptation
sources.population: People aged 18 years and over in hospital or long-term residential or nursing care.
sources.clinical_setting: Hospital and long-term care; the acute-hospital recommendations are partially applicable to emergency assessment.
sources.applicability_note: Exact only for recent fluctuating cognitive, perceptual, physical-function and social-behaviour changes, documentation of those changes, and use of a competent structured assessment. It does not establish delirium or cover every cause of altered mental status.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.1–1.3.1 — risk factors and indicators at presentation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting recent hours-to-days changes or fluctuations in cognition, perception, physical function and social behaviour reported by the person or an informant.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5.2–1.6.4 — document changes and competent assessment
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting observed changes and, when performed by a competent practitioner, the named structured assessment and clinician-confirmed diagnosis; no diagnosis is inferred by the template.

archive.publisher: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
archive.jurisdiction: England and Wales; requires UAE legal and pathway adaptation
archive.population: People aged 18 years and over in hospital or long-term residential or nursing care.
archive.setting: Hospital and long-term care; the acute-hospital recommendations are partially applicable to emergency assessment.

exact_relevant_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting observed changes and, when performed by a competent practitioner, the named structured assessment and clinician-confirmed diagnosis; no diagnosis is inferred by the template.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Medicines optimisation: the safe and effective use of medicines to enable the best possible outcomes — Recommendations
sources.jurisdiction: England and Wales; international evidence requiring UAE medicines, prescribing, pharmacy, discharge, and information-governance adaptation
sources.population: Adults, children, and young people using medicines, with specific transfer, reconciliation, and decision-involvement recommendations.
sources.clinical_setting: Transfers between care settings and clinician-led medicines consultations.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for documenting current medicines, changes made during transfer, reconciliation, discrepancies, person or carer involvement, values, preferences, and clinician-led decisions. It does not generate a medicine start, stop, dose, prescription, or deprescribing decision.

exact_sections.heading: Medicines-related communication between care settings
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports complete and accurate medicine information, prescribed and non-prescribed medicines, allergies, timing, monitoring, and discussion at care transitions.

exact_sections.heading: Medicines reconciliation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Defines reconciliation as identifying an accurate current medicine list, comparing records, recognising discrepancies, documenting changes, and including over-the-counter or complementary medicines.

exact_sections.heading: Medication review
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports structured review of all medicines, views, understanding, concerns, questions, adherence context, adverse-reaction or side-effect context, monitoring, and circumstances for seeking professional advice.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.2.2–1.3.7 — transfer information and medicines reconciliation
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting current prescribed, over-the-counter and complementary medicines, changes, discrepancies, discharge information, reconciliation and patient or carer involvement at care transitions.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.6.1–1.6.5 — involvement, values, preferences, benefits, and harms
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports documenting the person’s desired involvement, values, preferences, and clinician-completed discussion of treatment options, benefits and harms without generating a medicine decision.

sources.issuing_organisation: National Institute for Health and Care Excellence
sources.exact_document_title: Suspected sepsis in people aged 16 or over: recognition, assessment and early management
sources.population: People aged 16 years and over with suspected sepsis, excluding people who are or have recently been pregnant.
sources.clinical_setting: Community, ambulance, acute hospital, and emergency assessment settings.
sources.applicability_note: Exact for suspected sepsis recognition and assessment in non-pregnant people aged 16 years and over; partial for a broad fever or suspected-infection workflow and not directly validated for UAE pathways.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.1–1.2 — recognition, suspected infection, source, and risk factors
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports suspected-infection and fever presentation documentation, source symptoms, risk factors, and recognition that sepsis can present with nonspecific illness.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.3 — history, observations, circulation, respiration, skin, urine output, and source examination
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports temperature, heart rate, respiratory rate, blood pressure, consciousness, oxygen saturation, skin signs, urine output, fever or rigors, and examination for the suspected infection source.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.5–1.6 — community and acute risk criteria
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports confusion, respiratory compromise, hypotension, tachycardia, oliguria, temperature abnormality, non-blanching or mottled skin, and immunosuppression as assessed risk features.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendation 1.7 — emergency transfer, monitoring, and information
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports emergency transfer or escalation for high-risk presentations and documented monitoring, warning signs, and how to seek care for lower-risk presentations.

exact_sections.heading: Recommendations 1.8 and 1.11 — source identification and investigations
exact_sections.evidence_summary: Supports clinician-selected blood and microbiology testing, urinalysis, chest radiography, and abdominal or pelvic imaging to identify an infection source.
